threats.title: Factory V4 Withdraw Drains Entire Custom Token Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: In AgentFactoryV4.withdraw(), when withdrawing a custom token application, the factory transfers its ENTIRE balanceOf the custom token to the proposer, not the tracked deposited amount (initialLP). If the factory holds additional tokens of the same type from other sources, the withdrawer receives all of them.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function uses `IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))` instead of tracking and returning the exact deposited initialLP amount. This creates an unintended windfall for the withdrawer at the expense of other token holders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(customToken).safeTransfer(application.proposer, IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))) sends entire balance instead of deposited amount

preconditions: Factory holds more of the custom token than what was deposited by this proposer
Application status is Active
Caller is proposer or WITHDRAW_ROLE

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: balanceOf(address(this)) used instead of tracked deposit amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No initialLP amount stored for custom token withdrawal; entire balance transferred
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-application deposit tracking for custom tokens

analysis_questions: Can multiple custom token applications use the same token simultaneously?
Can tokens arrive at factory via airdrops or direct transfers?
Is the initialLP amount stored anywhere for later reference?
validation_requirements: Test: two applications with same custom token, one withdraws, verify it gets both deposits
Verify initialLP is not tracked in Application struct

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If factory holds extra custom tokens (from airdrops, dust, or concurrent applications), the withdrawer receives all of them. With the same token used in multiple applications, first withdrawer drains all.
prerequisites: Factory holds excess of the custom token beyond single deposit

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of custom tokens belonging to other applications or sent to factory
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Other proposers using the same custom token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multi-application withdrawal with same custom token

threats.title: Bot Protection Duration Set But Never Enforced in Transfer Logic
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentToken stores botProtectionDurationInSeconds during initialization but never references it in _transfer, _pretaxValidationAndLimits, or any other transfer-related function. The intended anti-bot protection after initial liquidity addition is completely absent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable is set from supply parameters and stored, but no transfer function checks `block.timestamp < fundedDate + botProtectionDurationInSeconds`. Bots can freely snipe token launches immediately after addInitialLiquidity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: botProtectionDurationInSeconds = supplyParams.botProtectionDurationInSeconds; variable is set but never read in any transfer path

preconditions: botProtectionDurationInSeconds > 0 configured
Initial liquidity just added

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: AgentToken.sol line 99 (set) and lines 385-410 (_pretaxValidationAndLimits - no check)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State variable set but never read in any code path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Dead code indicates missing security feature
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bot protection check in transfer logic

analysis_questions: Was maxTokensPerWallet/maxTokensPerTxn also intended but not implemented?
Is bot protection handled in a separate contract not in scope?
What is the economic impact of unprotected launches?
validation_requirements: Search all code for botProtectionDurationInSeconds usage beyond initialization
Confirm no external contract enforces this via _beforeTokenTransfer hook

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Bot monitors for addInitialLiquidity events. Immediately after event, bot buys large amount of tokens at initial price. Regular users buy at inflated prices. Bot sells for profit.
prerequisites: Token launched with bot protection configured

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Sniping bots extract value from every token launch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Loss is permanent; no mechanism to reverse snipe trades

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying bot protection enforcement during transfer

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient to buy tokens at launch
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large buys right after liquidity add are observable

threats.title: Tax Rate Above 100% Causes Unchecked Underflow in _taxProcessing
threats.hypothesis_description: setProjectTaxRates has no upper bound validation. If projectBuyTaxBasisPoints or projectSellTaxBasisPoints is set >= BP_DENOM (10000), the computed tax in _taxProcessing exceeds sentAmount_. The entire _taxProcessing block is unchecked, so `amountLessTax_ -= tax` underflows to near uint256.max, minting astronomical tokens to the recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked block at _taxProcessing means if tax > sentAmount_, the subtraction wraps. The recipient receives ~2^256 tokens. This is a catastrophic inflation bug triggered by a missing input validation in setProjectTaxRates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Entire _taxProcessing in unchecked block; amountLessTax_ -= tax underflows if tax > sentAmount_; no BP_DENOM cap on tax rates in setProjectTaxRates

preconditions: Owner or factory sets tax basis points >= 10000
A buy or sell transaction occurs through LP

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked { amountLessTax_ -= tax; } where tax can exceed sentAmount_
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: setProjectTaxRates accepts any uint16 value (up to 65535 BP = 655%) without validation

analysis_questions: Can the factory (not just owner) set tax rates? What constraints does factory have?
Would the underflowed amountLessTax_ cause _balances[to] += to exceed totalSupply?
What is the blast radius of infinite token inflation?
validation_requirements: Set tax to 10001 BP, execute a sell, verify amountLessTax_ underflows
Confirm _balances[to] inflates without totalSupply tracking
Verify no SafeMath wrapper on the unchecked block

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner/factory calls setProjectTaxRates(10001, 10001). On next sell to LP: tax = amount * 10001 / 10000 > amount. amountLessTax_ underflows to ~2^256. Recipient balance inflates to near-max uint256.
prerequisites: Owner or factory sets tax rate >= 10000 BP

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete destruction of token economy; infinite token minting

threats.test_coverage_gap: No boundary test for tax rates near or above BP_DENOM

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Two transactions: setProjectTaxRates + any LP trade

threats.title: Sentinel Value Collision Enables Duplicate Custom Token Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentFactoryV4 uses `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0` as sentinel for 'not registered'. If _nextId is initialized to 0, the first initFromToken creates application id=0, setting _tokenApplication[token]=0 which is indistinguishable from 'not registered'. This allows the same token to be registered twice.
threats.investigation_rationale: The V4 initialize takes nextId_ parameter. If passed 0, the first application ID is 0. _tokenApplication maps to 0, same as the default 'empty' value. A second initFromToken with the same token passes the check.
threats.config_evidence: No V4-specific deployment script found; V3 personaFactoryArguments.js does not include nextId_ for V4

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0) uses 0 as sentinel; _nextId starts at nextId_ which can be 0; _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] = id where id = 0

preconditions: _nextId initialized to 0
Attacker registers a custom token with the first application (id=0)
Attacker registers the same token again

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sentinel value 0 collides with valid application ID 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] = id where id=0 is same as default mapping value
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(_nextId > 0) or use of type(uint256).max as sentinel

analysis_questions: What value is _nextId initialized to in production?
Can the same token be double-registered and both withdrawn?
Does the second withdraw drain the first application's tokens?
validation_requirements: Initialize V4 with nextId_=0, register same token twice, verify both succeed
Test withdrawal of both applications

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: With _nextId=0, attacker calls initFromToken(tokenA, ...). Application 0 created, _tokenApplication[tokenA]=0. Attacker calls initFromToken(tokenA, ...) again - check passes since 0==0. Two applications exist for same token; both withdrawals try to drain factory balance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Double registration allows draining factory's balance of the custom token twice
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Factory and other custom token depositors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Second withdraw would fail if first drained all tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Duplicate NewApplication events for same token

threats.title: AgentFactoryV2 Uses Unsafe ERC20 Transfer for Initial Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentFactory (V2) executeApplication uses `IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount)` instead of SafeERC20.safeTransfer. If assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false on failure instead of reverting, the transfer silently fails and executeApplication proceeds with zero liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: V3 and V4 correctly use safeTransfer, but V2 uses raw IERC20.transfer. If assetToken doesn't revert on failure, the token contract receives 0 pairToken but addInitialLiquidity still executes, creating a broken LP pool with no base currency.
deployment_assumptions: assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false instead of reverting on failed transfer
threats.config_evidence: Deployment uses BRIDGED_TOKEN as assetToken; if this is WETH or standard ERC20, issue may not manifest. No evidence of assetToken type in deployment scripts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount) without SafeERC20 wrapper; V3/V4 use safeTransfer for same operation

preconditions: assetToken returns false on failed transfer instead of reverting
executeApplication is called with insufficient assetToken balance or other failure condition

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Non-reverting false return silently skips liquidity funding
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: SafeERC20 used elsewhere in contract but not for this critical transfer

analysis_questions: What is the actual assetToken used in production V2 deployment?
Does the assetToken revert or return false on failure?
Is V2 still actively used or superseded by V3/V4?
validation_requirements: Test with a token that returns false on transfer failure
Verify LP pool creation with 0 base currency

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If assetToken silently fails, executeApplication creates an LP pool with agent tokens but 0 base currency. Initial LP price is essentially 0, and the first buyer gets all tokens for free.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Broken LP pool; application deposit lost; tokens distributed at wrong price
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Application proposer and initial token buyers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: LP pool cannot be easily corrected once created

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with non-standard ERC20 return values

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: LP pool with 0 base currency visible on-chain

threats.title: _mint Uses uint128 Truncation for _totalSupply While _balances Uses uint256
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentToken._mint uses `_totalSupply += uint128(amount)` which silently truncates amounts exceeding uint128.max. The supply validation checks `maxSupply > type(uint128).max` in pre-decimal units, but actual minted amounts are `maxSupply * 10^18`. For maxSupply > ~3.4e20, the scaled amount exceeds uint128.max, causing _totalSupply to diverge from sum(_balances).
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation checks maxSupply before decimal scaling. A maxSupply of 1e21 passes the check but when scaled by 1e18 becomes 1e39, exceeding uint128.max (~3.4e38). _totalSupply silently wraps while _balances track the full uint256 amount, breaking conservation invariant.
deployment_assumptions: maxSupply configured > ~3.4e20 tokens (unusual but allowed by validation)
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts show AGENT_TOKEN_SUPPLY/AGENT_TOKEN_LIMIT env vars; typical values unknown

proof_of_signal.pattern: _totalSupply += uint128(amount) while _balances[account] += amount (uint256); validation checks maxSupply before 10^18 scaling

preconditions: Admin sets maxSupply to a value where maxSupply * 10^18 > type(uint128).max
maxSupply itself is <= type(uint128).max (passes the check)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uint128 truncation on _totalSupply while _balances uses uint256
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Validation at line 129 checks pre-decimal maxSupply against uint128.max; post-decimal amount can overflow uint128

analysis_questions: What are typical maxSupply values in production?
Would truncated _totalSupply affect autoswap threshold calculations?
Could divergence between _totalSupply and sum(_balances) be exploited?
validation_requirements: Set maxSupply = 1e21, verify _totalSupply truncation after _mint
Compare _totalSupply with sum of all _balances
Test autoswap behavior with truncated _totalSupply

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: With maxSupply = 1e21, actual minted amount is 1e39. _totalSupply wraps to ~6.58e2 (truncated). swapThresholdInTokens = (_totalSupply * swapThresholdBasisPoints) / BP_DENOM becomes tiny, triggering autoswap on every transaction. _burn may also malfunction.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Broken supply accounting; autoswap threshold miscalculation; potential token economic collapse
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of affected agent token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with maxSupply near uint128 boundary after decimal scaling

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: totalSupply() returns truncated value; visible on-chain

threats.title: DoS on Custom Token Application via Pre-Created Uniswap Pair
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentFactoryV4._createPair requires `factory.getPair(tokenAddr, assetToken) == address(0)` and reverts if the pair already exists. An attacker who observes a custom token application on-chain can directly create the Uniswap pair via the factory before the proposer calls executeTokenApplication, permanently blocking execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: After initFromToken is called, the custom token address is publicly known. An attacker can call Uniswap's createPair() for that token and assetToken. When the proposer later calls executeTokenApplication, _createPair reverts because the pair already exists. The proposer can only withdraw, losing the opportunity to create the agent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(factory.getPair(tokenAddr, assetToken) == address(0), 'pool already exists') - attacker can create pair first using observable on-chain data

preconditions: Custom token application exists (visible on-chain)
Uniswap pair for customToken/assetToken does not yet exist
Attacker calls Uniswap factory.createPair before proposer executes

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Strict require on pair non-existence; no fallback to use existing pair
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can create Uniswap pairs permissionlessly
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No handling of pre-existing pairs; should use getPair and only create if needed

analysis_questions: Can the proposer still withdraw after execution is DOSed?
Is there an admin function to resolve this state?
Could the factory use the existing pair instead of requiring creation?
validation_requirements: Create pair externally, verify executeTokenApplication reverts
Verify withdraw still works for DOSed application

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker monitors NewApplication events. When custom token application appears, attacker calls UniswapV2Factory.createPair(customToken, assetToken). Proposer's executeTokenApplication permanently reverts. Proposer can only withdraw, losing the opportunity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct fund loss (proposer can withdraw), but permanent DoS on agent creation for that token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Proposer can withdraw deposit but cannot create agent with that token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pre-existing pair scenario

threats.title: Unrestricted validator registration enables score inflation + unbounded validator set growth (governance scoring corruption / potential DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentNftV2.addValidator() is permissionless and, via ValidatorRegistry, initializes a new validator’s base score to the current max score (totalProposals). An attacker can mass-register validators late (after many proposals) to obtain inflated validatorScore and can also bloat the validator list, making any on-chain logic that iterates validators potentially gas-infeasible.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct, on-chain, unprivileged action that can distort validator ranking/selection and may break any downstream components that depend on validatorScore/totalUptimeScore. It also sets up a storage/gas griefing vector due to unbounded array growth per virtualId.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addValidator(uint256,address) has no access control; calls _addValidator + _initValidatorScore

preconditions: virtualId exists (virtualInfos[virtualId].dao may be set)
Attacker can submit transactions (no special roles required)
Downstream logic (off-chain or on-chain) uses validatorScore/validators list for rewards/selection

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless registration + base score assignment on registration
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _initValidatorScore sets base score using _getMaxScore(virtualId) which points to totalProposals
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No role check, no cap on validators array growth, no fee/bond for registration

analysis_questions: What components (on-chain or backend) use validatorScore or totalUptimeScore to allocate rewards or authority?
Can attacker meaningfully affect governance outcomes or validator selection by inflating scores?
Is totalUptimeScore called from any state-changing paths (not shown here) that could be DoSed?
validation_requirements: Simulate late registration after many proposals and measure validatorScore advantage
Stress-test validator list growth and gas usage for any functions consuming validatorAt/validatorCount
Trace reward/governance logic (AgentDAO/Reward contracts) that depend on these scores (out of provided scope but required for impact validation)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker waits until an agent DAO has a high proposalCount, then calls addValidator(virtualId, attacker) (and/or many sybil addresses). Each new validator gets base score ~= totalProposals, jumping to top of leaderboard and potentially capturing validator rewards or governance influence; additionally, the attacker grows validators array to make validator-iteration operations impractical.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High if validator scores drive emissions/rewards or selection; otherwise reputational/system integrity impact
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All stakers and participants in affected virtualId governance/rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Hard; would require admin migration/cleanup not present (no validator removal)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing tests asserting addValidator is restricted and base-score cannot be farmed

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Estimate validator list size where any validator-iteration function becomes non-executable
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain: spikes in NewValidator events; unusually large validator arrays

threats.title: VeToken Delegation Hijacking via Unrestricted stake() Receiver Parameter
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentVeToken.stake() allows any caller to specify an arbitrary `receiver` for minted veTokens and forcibly overrides that receiver's entire delegation to a caller-chosen `delegatee`. An attacker can call stake(1, victim, attackerValidator) to redirect ALL of victim's accumulated voting power to their controlled validator, at the cost of only 1 LP token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _delegate(receiver, delegatee) call operates on the FULL balance of receiver, not just the newly minted amount. This enables wholesale governance takeover for minimal cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) called with user-supplied receiver without verifying msg.sender == receiver; overrides entire balance delegation

preconditions: canStake == true (public agent) OR totalSupply == 0
Attacker holds >= 1 LP token (the staking asset)
Victim has existing veToken balance with delegation to a different validator
Agent is not blacklisted

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) in stake() overrides delegation for receiver's ENTIRE balance, not just the new amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls stake(1, victim, attackerDelegatee) to redirect victim's full voting power
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains victim's entire governance voting power for 1 LP token; can control DAO proposals, set DAO address, change agent metadata

economic_questions: What is the total staked value delegable per agent?
Can the attacker cascade this across multiple victims to gain DAO supermajority?
Can the attacker use DAO control to call setDAO and permanently hijack governance?
validation_requirements: Confirm _delegate overwrites entire balance delegation via OZ ERC20VotesUpgradeable
Test atomic attack: stake(1, victim, attacker) in single tx
Verify no hidden check on receiver == msg.sender in _update or _delegate

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full governance control over agent worth potentially millions in token/LP value

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker needs minimal LP tokens obtainable from Uniswap
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after victim has staked and delegated
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; pure governance manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any veToken holder with existing delegation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All delegated governance power per agent

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm voting power transfer is immediate and total

threats.title: AgentFactoryV4 Complete Asset Drain via assetToken as Custom Token
threats.hypothesis_description: initFromToken allows passing assetToken (e.g., VIRTUAL) as the tokenAddr. On withdraw, the factory first transfers withdrawableAmount of assetToken, then transfers the ENTIRE remaining assetToken balance (via IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))). Since customToken == assetToken, this drains all assetToken from the factory, stealing deposits from all other active applications.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no check preventing tokenAddr == assetToken. The isCompatibleToken check only verifies ERC20 interface compliance. The withdraw function sends the full balance of the custom token, not the tracked deposit amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw transfers IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) without checking customToken != assetToken; initFromToken has no guard against tokenAddr == assetToken

preconditions: Factory is not paused
Attacker has applicationThreshold + 1 wei of assetToken
assetToken implements standard ERC20 (name/symbol/totalSupply/balanceOf)
_tokenApplication[assetToken] == 0 (assetToken not previously registered)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw sends IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) which is the ENTIRE factory balance when customToken == assetToken
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call initFromToken(assetToken, ..., 1), then withdraw(id) in separate tx after proposalEndBlock
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Net profit = factory's entire pre-existing assetToken balance (all other applications' deposits)

economic_questions: How much assetToken does the factory typically hold from active applications?
Can multiple attackers race to exploit this?
Does block.number > proposalEndBlock pass immediately since proposalEndBlock = block.number?
validation_requirements: Confirm assetToken passes isCompatibleToken check
Test full initFromToken -> withdraw flow with tokenAddr = assetToken
Verify proposalEndBlock timing allows same-block or next-block withdrawal

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire factory assetToken balance (potentially hundreds of thousands of USD)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Effectively infinite ROI since capital is returned

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When factory holds maximum active application deposits
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct theft with no market manipulation needed

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all active application deposits in the factory

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: applicationThreshold value and number of active applications
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test that withdraw(proposalEndBlock == block.number) is exploitable next block

threats.title: Gas-Based DoS on totalUptimeScore via Mass Validator Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: ValidatorRegistry.totalUptimeScore() iterates over ALL validators for a virtualId in an unbounded loop. Combined with the permissionless addValidator (ECON-H003), an attacker can register thousands of dummy validators, making totalUptimeScore and any dependent functions revert due to block gas limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop in totalUptimeScore calls validatorScore for each validator, which makes external calls to the DAO. Thousands of validators would easily exceed the block gas limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded for loop over validatorCount(virtualId) calling validatorScore which involves external calls

preconditions: Target agent exists
totalUptimeScore is used by dependent protocol logic (rewards, ELO)
Attacker has gas for registering ~1000+ validators

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalUptimeScore iterates all validators with external call per iteration; gas cost grows linearly
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Register 5000+ unique addresses as validators via addValidator
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Denial of service on governance scoring and any reward distribution using totalUptimeScore

economic_questions: What functions depend on totalUptimeScore?
Can this permanently brick an agent's governance?
What is the cost of registering enough validators to exceed gas limit?
validation_requirements: Estimate gas per validatorScore call (includes DAO external call)
Calculate validators needed to exceed 30M gas block limit
Verify validators array grows unboundedly

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.5-2 ETH for gas on Base to register thousands of validators
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing attack - no direct profit but disrupts agent operations

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Anytime
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Indirect - disrupts governance and scoring

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All participants of the targeted agent
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Agent functionality and dependent reward systems

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test with 1000+ validators to confirm DoS

threats.title: Zero Slippage Protection on Tax Autoswap Enables Atomic Value Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentToken._swapTax passes amountOutMin=0 to swapExactTokensForTokensSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens, providing zero slippage protection. An attacker can atomically manipulate the pool price and trigger autoswap within a single transaction, extracting tax revenue that should go to the project tax recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: The autoswap is triggered during any non-LP transfer when accumulated tax exceeds the threshold. An attacker can craft a transaction that first manipulates pool reserves then triggers a transfer that causes autoswap at the manipulated price.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swapExactTokensForTokensSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens called with amountOutMin=0; any output accepted

preconditions: Token has tax enabled (_tokenHasTax == true)
Accumulated tax balance >= swap threshold
Pool has sufficient liquidity for manipulation
Attacker can trigger autoswap via non-LP transfer in same tx as manipulation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountOutMin = 0 means swap accepts any output, including near-zero after manipulation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Atomic tx: manipulate pool reserves → transfer triggers autoswap at manipulated price → reverse manipulation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Capture the difference between fair-value and manipulated-price swap output

economic_questions: What is the typical accumulated tax balance before autoswap triggers?
Can the attacker atomically manipulate price and trigger autoswap in one tx?
What pool depth is needed for profitable manipulation?

Calculate profit margins accounting for tax on attacker's trades
Verify _eligibleForSwap conditions during atomic attack

threats.title: Tax Rate Monotonicity Not Enforced Despite Documented Constraint
threats.hypothesis_description: setProjectTaxRates comment states rates are 'subject to only ever decreasing' but the code has no enforcement. Owner/factory can increase tax rates to 100% (10000 BP) at any time, effectively trapping holders who can only sell at total loss. This is a code bug: the documented invariant is not implemented.
threats.investigation_rationale: Token holders rely on the stated property that tax rates can only decrease. The lack of enforcement allows rug-pull via tax rate manipulation. A launch at 2% tax attracting buyers, followed by increase to 99%, would devastate holders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Comment says 'subject to only ever decreasing' but no check: newProjectBuyTaxBasisPoints_ <= projectBuyTaxBasisPoints is missing

preconditions: Owner or factory calls setProjectTaxRates with higher values
Token has been launched with lower tax rates attracting holders

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No comparison between old and new rates; direct assignment: projectBuyTaxBasisPoints = newProjectBuyTaxBasisPoints_
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Launch with 1% tax, attract holders, then set tax to 99%
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Tax recipient captures 99% of all sell transactions

economic_questions: How many tokens have been launched with this code?
What is the typical token TVL at risk?
Is there any external enforcement mechanism?
validation_requirements: Confirm no require/assert checking old >= new rates
Test setting rates from 100 to 5000 BP
Verify increased tax applies immediately to next trade

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of all sell volume captured as tax
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high on accumulated sell volume

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant holder accumulation
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Token price collapse as holders cannot sell without massive tax

threats.title: Bonding Curve Creator Front-running to Steal Application Threshold
threats.hypothesis_description: When `initFromBondingCurve` is called by the `BONDING_ROLE`, it deposits `applicationThreshold_` from the caller but sets the `creator` parameter as the application's `proposer`. The `creator` can immediately front-run the execution to withdraw and steal the deposited funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentFactoryV3.sol`, `initFromBondingCurve` transfers `assetToken` from `msg.sender` (the BONDING_ROLE contract) but assigns ownership of the application to `creator`. Because `withdraw` only requires `msg.sender == application.proposer`, the `creator` can call `withdraw(id)` to extract the funds paid by the bonding curve contract, causing subsequent execution to fail.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Sender pays threshold but creator is set as proposer who can withdraw

preconditions: BONDING_ROLE contract calls initFromBondingCurve on behalf of a user (creator)
Application remains in Active state briefly before execution

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw() sends withdrawableAmount to proposer (creator)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Creator monitors mempool and calls withdraw() before executeBondingCurveApplication is called
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Creator steals the applicationThreshold_ (e.g., 1000 VIRTUAL) paid by the bonding curve protocol.

economic_questions: Does the BONDING_ROLE contract execute the application in the same transaction, or is there a delay?
If atomic, can the creator reenter during the bonding curve flow?
validation_requirements: Check the external FGenesis contract flow to see if execution is atomic with initialization.

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Asset Drain via Application Withdrawal
threats.hypothesis_description: If the configured `assetToken` has a fee-on-transfer mechanism, `proposeAgent` records the full `applicationThreshold` as `withdrawableAmount` even though the factory receives less. An attacker can repeatedly propose and withdraw to drain the factory's `assetToken` balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentFactoryV4.sol`, `proposeAgent` uses `safeTransferFrom` to pull `applicationThreshold` but does not verify the actual amount received by checking balances before and after. It then sets `withdrawableAmount = applicationThreshold`. When `withdraw` is called, the full `applicationThreshold` is sent out. The difference is paid out of other users' deposits.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts use process.env.ASSET_TOKEN. If this token implements fees, the vulnerability exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom used without balance checks, withdrawableAmount hardcoded

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls proposeAgent then withdraw in a loop
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker drains the factory's assetToken reserves by the fee amount per loop iteration, causing insolvency.

Can the attacker profit if the fee goes to a burn address vs a treasury they control?

threats.title: Factory application withdrawal can be forced to mature immediately, enabling instant deposit recycling/spam and operational DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: In AgentFactoryV2/V3/V4, proposeAgent sets `proposalEndBlock = block.number` (comment says no longer required). withdraw requires `block.number > proposalEndBlock`, which becomes true the very next block. This allows an attacker to rapidly propose and withdraw, cycling the applicationThreshold deposit with minimal capital lock, spamming NewApplication events and bloating operational processing/monitoring (and potentially any off-chain indexing/backends that process applications).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete workflow defect: the maturity gate is effectively a 1-block delay, undermining any intended friction. Operationally, this can degrade UX, overwhelm off-chain pipelines, and create noisy state for any systems iterating applications.

threats.title: AgentMigrator uses raw ERC20 transferFrom which can silently fail for non-standard tokens, causing broken migrations and stuck workflows
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentMigrator.migrateAgent funds initial liquidity by calling `IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount)` without SafeERC20. For non-standard ERC20s that return `false` instead of reverting, the call may silently fail, yet migration proceeds to addInitialLiquidity and later staking. This can produce an agent with no (or insufficient) paired liquidity, broken pricing, and/or revert in subsequent steps, leading to operational failures and potential fund loss if subsequent steps assume liquidity exists.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete integration defect. While Base's primary assets are typically standard, the migrator is configurable and may be used with bridged or non-standard tokens (docs mention bridged token).
deployment_assumptions: _assetToken configured in AgentMigrator can be a non-standard ERC20 (returns false on failure) or a token that can fail transferFrom without revert
Founder may not have sufficient allowance/balance at time of migration triggering failure
threats.config_evidence: v2/deployMigrator.ts sets assetToken via setInitParams(process.env.BRIDGED_TOKEN) without asserting standard ERC20 semantics

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount); // not SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom

preconditions: Migrator is configured with an asset token that does not reliably revert on failed transfers OR can return false
Migration is attempted with insufficient allowance/balance or token-specific transfer restrictions

threats.title: Permanent DAO Hijacking via setDAO
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setDAO` function allows the current DAO to change the DAO address to any arbitrary contract without validation. If an attacker gains temporary control of the DAO, they can permanently lock it to their own contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentNftV2.sol`, `setDAO` requires the caller to be the current DAO. If an attacker successfully executes the delegation overwrite attack (TECH-H001) to gain majority voting power, they can pass a proposal that calls `setDAO(virtualId, attackerContract)`. Because there is no validation that `newDAO` is a legitimate governor contract, the attacker's contract becomes the permanent DAO. They can then unilaterally control all DAO-gated functions (like `setCoreTypes` and `setTokenURI`) forever.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; missing validation on critical state update.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Critical address update without interface or legitimacy validation

preconditions: Attacker gains majority voting power in the current DAO (e.g., via TECH-H001)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: `info.dao = newDAO;` accepts any address, including EOAs or malicious contracts
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should verify `newDAO` implements `IGovernor` or restrict changes to a factory-approved list

analysis_questions: Can `setDAO` be chained with the delegation overwrite to achieve permanent takeover?
What is the impact of controlling `setCoreTypes` and `setTokenURI`?
validation_requirements: Simulate a DAO proposal calling `setDAO`
Verify the new DAO can execute restricted functions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker steals voting power using TECH-H001. They create a proposal to call `setDAO` with their own EOA. Once passed, the attacker directly calls `setTokenURI` to deface the agent NFT and `setCoreTypes` to disrupt agent functionality, holding the agent hostage.
prerequisites: Ability to pass a DAO proposal

threats.title: AgentFactoryV4 execute path leaves custom-token application mappings uncleared, creating stuck tokenAddr state and potential fund recovery issues
threats.hypothesis_description: In AgentFactoryV4, when a custom token application is executed, _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] and _applicationToken[id] are not cleared (they are only cleared on withdraw). This can permanently block re-applying with the same token address (because _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] != 0), even if the prior application is Executed and no longer needs the mapping. It also complicates recovery if execution partially fails after transfers/approvals but before completion.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a state machine correctness defect affecting protocol usability and potentially trapping user deposits if there are failure modes during custom token execution. It is attacker-relevant because griefers can intentionally create an application with a token to block others from using it again (if mapping is never cleared).

threats.title: Stale Balance Read in _transfer During AutoSwap Creates Phantom Token Inflation via distributeTaxTokens
threats.hypothesis_description: _transfer reads _balances[from] before calling _autoSwap. When from==address(this) (triggered via permissionless distributeTaxTokens), _autoSwap executes a Uniswap swap that calls back into transferFrom, which invokes a nested _transfer that modifies _balances[address(this)]. After the nested call returns, the outer _transfer overwrites _balances[address(this)] with the stale pre-swap value minus the distributeTaxTokens amount, erasing the deduction from the swap. This creates phantom tokens in the contract equal to the swap amount, inflating total balance sum without changing _totalSupply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _transfer function has a read-before-external-call-then-write pattern where the write uses a stale value. The _autoSwap triggers an external Uniswap router call that re-enters _transfer for address(this). The outer write at line _balances[from] = fromBalance - amount uses the pre-autoswap value, overwriting the inner write. This violates balance conservation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: fromBalance read at L389, _autoSwap called at L392 (modifies _balances[from] via re-entrant _transfer through Uniswap callback), then stale fromBalance used at L397: _balances[from] = fromBalance - amount

preconditions: _tokenHasTax == true (token configured with tax)
projectTaxPendingSwap > 0 (tax has accumulated from trading)
Contract token balance >= swap threshold (autoswap eligible)
Uniswap pair has liquidity for the swap to succeed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _balances[from] = fromBalance - amount at L397 uses fromBalance from L389, ignoring _autoSwap's nested _transfer that already modified _balances[address(this)]
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: distributeTaxTokens() calls _transfer(address(this), recipient, amount, false). _autoSwap triggers, sending swapBalance tokens to Uniswap via nested transferFrom→_transfer. Outer _transfer overwrites balance with stale value, creating phantom tokens = swapBalance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each call to distributeTaxTokens creates phantom tokens ≈ swapBalance. These phantom tokens persist in contract balance and are swapped to pairToken on next autoswap, draining value from Uniswap pool to projectTaxRecipient. Repeatable as tax re-accumulates.

economic_questions: What is the exact phantom token creation amount per distributeTaxTokens call?
How quickly does tax re-accumulate to enable repeated exploitation?
What is the cumulative value extracted from the Uniswap pool over N iterations?
Can the projectTaxRecipient's gained pairToken be quantified per cycle?
validation_requirements: Trace full execution: distributeTaxTokens → _transfer → _autoSwap → router.swap → nested transferFrom → _transfer → verify stale write
Confirm _autoSwapInProgress prevents tax in nested _transfer but does NOT prevent balance modification
Calculate phantom token amount = swapBalance (amount swapped in autoswap)
Verify sum(_balances) > _totalSupply after exploitation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Phantom tokens created ≈ swapBalance per call. Value extracted = market value of swapBalance tokens from LP
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction per cycle; repeatable after tax re-accumulates

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Call when projectTaxPendingSwap > 0 and contract balance >= swap threshold
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Each cycle extracts value from LP proportional to swapBalance; compounds over time

threats.title: AgentToken.setSwapThresholdBasisPoints Has No Upper Bound Enabling Tax Token Lock or Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: setSwapThresholdBasisPoints accepts any uint16 value (up to 65535 BP = 655%). An extremely high value makes swapThresholdInTokens = (_totalSupply * 65535) / 10000 which could exceed the total supply, permanently preventing autoswap. Conversely, setting it to 0 would trigger autoswap on every single transfer (when the contract has any balance), creating excessive gas consumption and potential DoS. Neither extreme is validated.
threats.investigation_rationale: The swap threshold directly controls when accumulated tax tokens are converted. Extreme values corrupt the autoswap mechanism, either trapping tax forever in the contract or causing gas-intensive swaps on every transfer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swapThresholdBasisPoints = swapThresholdBasisPoints_ with no bounds validation; threshold used in _autoSwap at L442

preconditions: Owner or factory calls setSwapThresholdBasisPoints with extreme value
_tokenHasTax == true

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: swapThresholdInTokens = (_totalSupply * swapThresholdBasisPoints) / BP_DENOM; at 65535 BP this equals ~6.5x totalSupply, unreachable
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Setting threshold to max permanently disables autoswap, trapping all tax tokens. Setting to 0 triggers swap on every transfer with >=1 token in contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Trapped tax tokens devalue the token (dead supply). Combined with distributeTaxTokens, could be exploited for selective distribution.

economic_questions: What is the practical impact of permanently disabled autoswap?

validation_requirements: Calculate swapThresholdInTokens at boundary values

threats.title: AgentFactoryV3 executeBondingCurveApplication Accepts Unvalidated Supply Parameters Enabling Extreme Token Configurations
threats.hypothesis_description: executeBondingCurveApplication constructs tokenSupplyParams from caller-provided totalSupply, lpSupply, and vault with no validation. The BONDING_ROLE can set totalSupply extremely high (triggering uint128 truncation in AgentToken._mint), set lpSupply=0 (no LP supply, breaking liquidity), set vault to an attacker-controlled address receiving totalSupply-lpSupply tokens, or create configurations where the AgentToken.processSupplyParams check passes but downstream mint truncates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function directly encodes caller-supplied values into the token supply parameters without checking reasonableness. Combined with the uint128 truncation bug in AgentToken._mint, this creates a direct path to supply corruption.

threats.title: AgentVeToken.setCanStake Founder Toggle Enables Governance Capture via Staking Lock-Out
threats.hypothesis_description: The founder can call setCanStake(false) at any time to disable public staking. After accumulating governance power via initial staking, the founder toggles canStake to false, preventing new stakers from diluting their voting power. The founder (or their delegatees) then maintain permanent governance majority without risk of being outvoted. They can toggle canStake back temporarily to allow selected allies to stake.
threats.investigation_rationale: The canStake boolean is a simple toggle with no cooldown, timelock, or governance vote required. Combined with the non-transferable nature of veTokens, this gives the founder unilateral control over who can participate in governance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_msgSender() == founder) — only founder check, no timelock or governance requirement

preconditions: Caller is the agent founder
Agent was initially set with canStake=true (public)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Founder stakes, delegates to ally validators, then disables public staking. No new governance participants can join.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Founder maintains permanent governance majority, can pass any proposals including diverting agent revenue or changing metadata

threats.title: AgentVeToken Admin Can Bypass Founder Lock by Setting matureAt to Past Timestamp
threats.hypothesis_description: setMatureAt() allows any address with ADMIN_ROLE on agentNft to set the matureAt timestamp to any value, including 0 or past timestamps. This bypasses the founder's initialLock intended to protect stakers by ensuring the founder's LP cannot be withdrawn for the maturity duration (e.g., 10 years).
threats.investigation_rationale: The trust model states that founders' initial LP stake is locked until maturity to reassure other stakers. The admin can unilaterally circumvent this by calling setMatureAt(0), enabling immediate founder withdrawal and potential LP dump.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setMatureAt has no require(_matureAt >= matureAt) check; allows reducing lock period or setting to 0

preconditions: Attacker has ADMIN_ROLE on agentNft contract
Agent has founder with initialLock that hasn't matured yet

threats.title: AgentMigrator Owner Reset Enables Re-Migration Stranding Existing VeToken Stakers

threats.title: AgentNftV2 setDAO Can Permanently Brick Agent Governance by Setting DAO to address(0)
threats.hypothesis_description: setDAO() allows the current DAO to change itself to any address including address(0), with no validation. If a malicious DAO proposal passes that calls setDAO(virtualId, address(0)), the agent's governance becomes permanently bricked since all onlyVirtualDAO-gated functions require msg.sender == address(0), which is impossible.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onlyVirtualDAO modifier checks _msgSender() == virtualInfos[virtualId].dao. If dao is set to address(0), no caller can ever satisfy this condition. setCoreTypes, setTokenURI, and setDAO itself all become permanently inaccessible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDAO performs no validation on newDAO address; require(_msgSender() == virtualInfos[virtualId].dao) in modifier becomes unsatisfiable if dao == address(0)

preconditions: Attacker gains DAO governance majority (via OPS-H001 delegation hijack or legitimate voting power)
Malicious proposal to call AgentNftV2.setDAO(virtualId, address(0)) passes

threats.title: AgentMigrator Uses Unsafe IERC20.transferFrom for Asset Token Transfer During Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentMigrator.migrateAgent uses raw IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount) without SafeERC20, despite importing SafeERC20 in other contracts. If _assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false on failure, the transfer silently fails and migration proceeds with zero initial liquidity, creating a broken agent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the same pattern as OPS-H007 in AgentFactoryV2 but in the migrator context. The deployer configures _assetToken via setInitParams, and if it's ever set to a non-standard ERC20, all migrations create broken LP pools.

threats.title: Tax Rate Underflow Enables Infinite Token Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setProjectTaxRates` function lacks an upper bound check. If an admin sets the tax rate above 100%, the unchecked subtraction in `_taxProcessing` underflows, minting massive amounts of tokens to the recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentToken.sol`, `setProjectTaxRates` allows the owner to set tax basis points to any `uint16` value (up to 65535, or 655%). If set above 10000, the calculated `tax` exceeds `sentAmount_`. In `_taxProcessing`, the code `amountLessTax_ -= tax` is inside an `unchecked` block. This causes `amountLessTax_` to underflow to near `type(uint256).max`. The recipient's balance is then increased by this massive amount, completely destroying the token's economics.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; logic flaw in bounds checking.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unchecked subtraction where subtrahend can be larger than minuend due to missing bounds check

preconditions: Admin or Factory calls `setProjectTaxRates` with a value > 10000
A transfer occurs that triggers the tax

analysis_questions: Is there any off-chain validation preventing the admin from setting high rates?
Does the underflow successfully update balances without reverting elsewhere?
validation_requirements: Set tax rate to 20000
Execute a transfer
Verify recipient receives ~uint256.max tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised admin key or malicious insider calls `setProjectTaxRates(20000, 20000)`. They then execute a small sell transaction. The underflow mints them trillions of tokens, which they immediately dump into the Uniswap pool, draining all paired liquidity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total drain of LP pool and destruction of token value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders and liquidity providers

threats.title: Infinite Token Minting via Tax Rate Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: If an admin sets the tax rate above 100%, the unchecked arithmetic in tax processing will underflow, minting near-infinite tokens to the recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentToken.sol`, `setProjectTaxRates` does not enforce an upper bound on `projectBuyTaxBasisPoints` or `projectSellTaxBasisPoints`. If an admin (or compromised admin key) sets the rate > 10,000 (100%), the calculated `tax` in `_taxProcessing` will be greater than `sentAmount_`. Because the subtraction `amountLessTax_ -= tax` occurs inside an `unchecked` block, it will underflow, resulting in `amountLessTax_` becoming a massive number (~uint256.max). This massive number is then added to the recipient's balance, effectively minting infinite tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountLessTax_ -= tax inside unchecked block with no upper bound on tax rate

A transfer occurs that triggers the tax

threats.title: AgentToken.withdrawERC20 Can Extract PairToken From Contract Breaking AutoSwap Economics
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawERC20 allows the owner/factory to withdraw ANY ERC20 except address(this). This includes pairToken (the LP pair currency). If pairToken accumulates in the contract (e.g., from failed swaps, direct transfers, or accounting errors), the owner can extract it. More critically, the infinite approval to the Uniswap router means the router could be exploited in combination to route pairToken through the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract gives the router infinite approval for pairToken in _addInitialLiquidity. If the router is ever upgraded or compromised, it could pull pairToken from the contract. Additionally, the owner can withdraw any pairToken that ends up in the contract regardless of its intended purpose.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (token_ == address(this)) revert; IERC20(token_).safeTransfer — only blocks self-token, allows pairToken extraction

threats.title: setDAO Allows Permanent Governance Hijack
threats.hypothesis_description: The setDAO function allows the current DAO to set a new DAO address without validation, enabling a compromised DAO to permanently lock out legitimate governance.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an attacker gains majority voting power in the DAO (e.g., via the delegation overwrite bug or Sybil validators), they can pass a proposal to call setDAO(virtualId, attackerContract) or setDAO(virtualId, address(0)). Because there is no validation on the new DAO address, this permanently hijacks or bricks all DAO-gated functions (like setCoreTypes and setTokenURI).
